name: Vaikuntha
description: Vaikuntha is the celestial abode of Lord Vishnu and his consort, Goddess Lakshmi, in Hindu mythology. It is considered the supreme abode, a realm of eternal bliss, and the ultimate destination for devotees who have attained moksha (liberation). Described as a place of unimaginable beauty and splendor, Vaikuntha is free from the cycle of birth and death, suffering, and ignorance. It is a realm of pure consciousness, characterized by eternal happiness, peace, and devotion. The inhabitants of Vaikuntha are eternally liberated souls (Muktas) who constantly engage in the loving service of Lord Vishnu. The environment is said to be filled with divine light, fragrant flowers, and celestial music. Vaikuntha represents the highest spiritual attainment, a state of perfect union with the divine. It is the antithesis of the material world, which is characterized by impermanence and suffering. Reaching Vaikuntha is the ultimate goal for many Hindus, representing the culmination of a life dedicated to dharma, karma, and bhakti (devotion). The concept of Vaikuntha provides a powerful vision of spiritual perfection and inspires devotees to strive for liberation from the cycle of samsara. It is also described as being self-illuminating and beyond the reach of material senses or comprehension, attainable only through divine grace and unwavering devotion.
subtitle: The Abode of Lord Vishnu

keyMyths.title: Reaching Vaikuntha
keyMyths.description: Devotees attain Vaikuntha through unwavering devotion and by performing righteous actions. Upon death, their souls, purified by bhakti, are granted entry into this eternal abode.

keyMyths.title: Description of Vaikuntha
keyMyths.description: Vaikuntha is depicted as a realm of unimaginable beauty, filled with divine light, fragrant flowers, and celestial music. It is a place where suffering and ignorance do not exist.

sources.author: Bibek Debroy
sources.description: Translation and commentary on the Bhagavad Gita
sources.text: Translation and commentary on the Bhagavad Gita
